greeting_text: Issue #4 of Sophie's World is here — packed with wild facts, big news from around the globe, and your trickiest challenge yet! 🌟

sections.title: 🤔 Weird But True
sections.render_title: You Won't Believe These Are Real!
sections.section_intro: Nature is seriously weird — and seriously awesome. 🌿

items.title: 🐙 Octopuses Have Three Hearts — And Blue Blood!
items.body: An octopus has three hearts pumping blue blood through its body. Two hearts push blood through its gills, and one heart pumps it everywhere else. Here's the wildest part: when an octopus swims, its main heart actually stops beating — which is why they prefer to crawl!

items.title: 🌳 Trees Talk to Each Other Underground
items.body: Forests have a secret internet! Trees send nutrients and warning signals to each other through a huge underground network of tiny fungi called the 'Wood Wide Web.' Big trees can even send food to baby trees through these hidden pathways.

items.title: 🦦 Sea Otters Hold Hands While They Sleep
items.body: So they don't drift away from each other, sea otters hold hands — or paws! — while snoozing on the water. A group of resting otters is called a 'raft.' Now you know why!

links.label: More Wild Facts — Nat Geo Kids

sections.title: 🌍 World Watch
sections.render_title: Big Stories From Around the Globe
sections.section_intro: Two major things are happening in the world right now — here's what you need to know!

items.headline: 💸 The Trade War Heats Up: What Are Tariffs and Why Do They Matter?
body: The United States and China are in a major disagreement about trade — the buying and selling of goods between countries. The US has added high taxes called tariffs on things it buys from China, like electronics, clothing, and toys. China has fired back with its own tariffs on American goods like farm products.

These extra taxes make products more expensive for regular families in both countries. Leaders are meeting to try to work out a deal, but so far it remains a tense standoff — and prices at stores are feeling it.
items.analogy: Imagine two kids in a trading-card game who both decide to charge each other double for every swap. Pretty soon nobody wants to trade, both kids end up with fewer cool cards, and everyone loses!

links.label: What Are Tariffs? — Time for Kids

items.headline: 🌡️ Scientists Confirm: Earth Just Had Its Hottest Year Ever
body: Scientists have confirmed that the past year was the hottest ever recorded on Earth. That is a big deal — it means our planet is warming up faster than expected. Heatwaves, wildfires, and rising sea levels are becoming more common all over the world.

Countries are working together on plans to use more clean energy like solar and wind power. Young people everywhere are speaking up about climate action, and scientists say the choices made in the next few years will really matter for everyone's future.
items.analogy: Think of Earth like a cozy blanket fort. Too many greenhouse gases are like adding too many extra blankets — it gets way too hot inside, and it's really hard to cool back down.

links.label: What Can Kids Do? — Time for Kids

sections.title: 🇸🇬 Singapore Spotlight
sections.render_title: The Greenest Little City on Earth

items.headline: 🌿 Half of Singapore Is Covered in Green — Here's How
body: Even though Singapore is one of the world's busiest and most crowded cities, almost half of it is covered in greenery! The country plants trees along every road, builds parks everywhere, and even grows gardens on the sides of skyscrapers. It's proudly nicknamed a 'City in a Garden.'

The famous Gardens by the Bay has giant Supertrees — tall metal structures completely covered in real living plants that glow and light up at night. Some Supertrees are taller than a 16-story building! Singapore proves that a city can be super modern and super green at the same time.

sections.title: 🇺🇸 USA Corner
sections.render_title: California Is Soaking Up the Sun — Literally

items.headline: ☀️ California's Solar Power Is Breaking Records!
body: California is leading the entire country in solar energy! On sunny days, the state generates so much electricity from solar panels that it can power millions of homes — including homes right here in the Bay Area near Fremont. In fact, some days California makes more solar power than it can use!

Solar panels keep getting cheaper, and more families across the state are putting them on their rooftops. California has set a bold goal to run on 100% clean energy by 2045. Scientists say the state is making great progress, and the sunny weather definitely helps!

links.label: California Clean Energy — Time for Kids

sections.title: 🤸 Gymnastics Corner
sections.render_title: Flips, Champions, and Skills to Try
sections.section_intro: Whether you're working on your cartwheel or dreaming big, this section is all yours! 🌟

items.headline: 🏅 Simone Biles: The Gymnast Who Changed Everything
body: Simone Biles is not just the most decorated gymnast in US history — she's widely considered the greatest gymnast who has ever lived. She has invented four skills so difficult that they were officially named after her. These moves are rated among the hardest in the entire sport.

What makes Simone extra amazing is that she also spoke openly about taking care of her mental health. She showed gymnasts everywhere that being truly brave means knowing when to rest and take care of yourself, not just landing the hardest flip. She is a champion inside and out.

items.headline: 💡 Skill Tip: Build a Beautiful Cartwheel
body: A great cartwheel starts with your arms — reach one hand to the floor, then the other, keeping both arms straight like a sturdy bridge. Your legs should make a big star shape at the top.
Practice slowly at first, even against a wall for balance. Focus on straight arms and pointed toes, and your cartwheel will start to look (and feel!) much more powerful over time.

sections.title: 💰 Money Moves
sections.render_title: How Money Grows While You Sleep

items.headline: 💡 Concept of the Week: The Magic of Compound Interest
body: Here's a money secret that most adults wish they learned when they were young: money can make more money while you sleep! It's called compound interest. When you put money in a savings account, the bank pays you a little extra called interest. Then next month, that interest earns even more interest. The amounts keep growing on top of each other.

If you saved just $5 a week starting right now, by the time you turn 18 you could have well over $2,600 — and that's before the interest is even added in. The earlier you start, the more powerful this gets!
items.analogy: It's like a snowball rolling down a snowy hill — the longer it rolls, the bigger it gets all on its own. Your savings work the same way!

items.headline: 🧁 Kid Entrepreneur Spotlight: Cory Nieves, the Cookie CEO
body: When Cory Nieves was just 6 years old, he started selling hot cocoa outside his apartment building to help his mom save up for a car. His little business grew so fast that by age 10, he launched his own cookie brand called Mr. Cory's Cookies. Big stores and TV shows soon came knocking!

Cory always says: 'The key to success is to never give up and to enjoy what you do.' He now helps other young kids learn about starting their own businesses. Not bad for someone who started with a thermos and a dream!

sections.title: 🧩 Weekly Challenge
sections.render_title: Can You Crack This Week's Puzzle?

items.prompt_intro: This week in World Watch, we learned that the US and China are adding tariffs — extra taxes that make products more expensive. Let's see if you can figure out what that means for real shoppers at the store!
items.prompt: A toy normally costs $20. The US adds a 25% tariff, which means the price goes up by 25%. What is the new price of the toy? If a family buys 3 of these toys at the new price, how much extra money do they spend compared to if there were no tariff?
items.bonus: China adds a 15% tariff on American apples that normally cost $4 each. What is the new price of one apple?
items.hint: To find 25% of $20, divide 20 by 4 — that gives you one quarter of the amount. Add that to the original $20 to get the new price. Then multiply to find the extra cost for 3 toys!

links.label: What Are Tariffs? — Time for Kids
